pattern: The largest all-ones square ending at a cell is one larger than the smallest of the squares ending immediately above, left, and up-left, so a single dynamic-programming sweep finds the maximum side and its area is the answer.
hints: The answer is an area, so track the largest square side you can place with its bottom-right corner at each cell.
A cell containing '1' can extend a square only as far as its top, left, and top-left neighbours all allow.
Set dp[i][j] = 1 + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]) when the cell is '1' (else 0); the maximum side squared is the answer.

constraints.note: each entry is '0' or '1'; 1 <= rows, cols <= 300

edge_inputs.description: a 2x2 square exists within a larger grid
no 2x2 block, best is a single cell
a 2x2 broken by one zero on the diagonal
single row cannot form a square larger than one

stress.description: random 0/1 grid with a bias toward ones so larger squares appear
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    m = rng.randint(1, max(1, min(60, size)))
    n = rng.randint(1, max(1, min(60, size)))
    matrix = [[ '1' if rng.random() < 0.6 else '0' for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(m)]
    return [matrix]
stress.note: size capped at 60 so the cubic brute oracle remains fast; bias creates non-trivial squares
